title: Cool the heat
description: Anger and anxiety are loud — here's how to turn the volume down.
summaryPoints: Read anger and anxiety as information, not as orders to obey
Catch the early body signs — heat, clenched jaw, racing chest
Slow your exhale to tell your nervous system you're safe
Never decide or send a message while you're still hot
Check your story against the facts before you react

content.title: Red zone
content.body:
Someone gets the credit for your work. Your chest tightens, your jaw locks, your face goes hot. That's anger and anxiety doing the exact same thing — flooding you with adrenaline so you can fight or run.

They live in the same corner of the mood meter: **high energy, unpleasant**. The red zone. Loud, fast, and very sure it's right.

The flood isn't the problem. Acting from it is.
content.imageAlt: A thermometer cooling from hot to calm

content.title: They're messengers
content.body:
Anger isn't a flaw. It's a flag: **a boundary got crossed, or something feels unfair**. That's useful data.

Anxiety isn't a flaw either. It's an alarm: **your brain spotted a possible threat** and hit the panic button early, just in case.

The goal was never to stop feeling them. It's to hear the message without letting the messenger grab the wheel.

content.prompt: Anger is a bad emotion you should get rid of.
content.explanation: Anger is information — it flags an unfair or crossed-line moment. The skill is feeling it without acting from the flood.

content.prompt: Place each feeling on the mood meter.

items.text: Furious your sibling read your messages without asking
Panicked you'll bomb the test you didn't study for
Hyped the second your team scores the winner
Flat and heavy after a long, draining day
Calm and easy on a slow Sunday morning

content.explanation: Anger and anxiety both sit in the red — high energy, unpleasant. Same body, same corner; the calm comes from how you respond.

content.title: Cooling anger
content.body:
Anger moves fast, so catch it early — the heat in your face, the louder voice, the clenched fists.

Then:
- **Slow your breathing** to take your foot off the gas
- **Step away** if you can, even for a minute
- **Never decide or send anything while hot** — the text can wait

The hot version of you wants to win the moment. The cool version of you has to live with what it sends.

content.instructions: One word makes this advice backfire. Tap it.
content.sentence: When anger spikes, the smartest move is to reply instantly.
content.explanation: Never send a message while you are hot — give it time so the adrenaline can drain first.

content.title: Check your story
content.body:
Anger and anxiety both come with a story attached, and the story is usually exaggerated.

Anger says they did that on purpose. Anxiety says this will be a total disaster. Maybe — but maybe not.

**Reappraise**: Is this actually worth it? Is my story even accurate? A friend who 'ignored' you might just have a dead phone. Catching the inflated story takes the air out of the feeling.

content.prompt: What's the move that actually cools the anger down?
content.context: A group member takes full credit for the project in front of everyone. Your face goes hot.
options: Take a slow breath, step away, and sort it out once you're calm
Reply-all to the whole group calling them out right now
Say nothing ever and let it quietly eat at you for weeks
Fire back a sarcastic comment so they know you noticed
content.explanation: Breathing and stepping away drains the adrenaline so you can address it clearly — not silence, and not a hot reaction you'll regret.

content.title: Calming anxiety
content.body:
Anxiety is your alarm misfiring — the threat usually isn't as big as it screams. You can talk your nervous system down.

**Box breathing**: in for 4, hold 4, out 4, hold 4. The long, slow exhale is the signal for you're safe.

Then **ground in the present** with your senses — five things you can see, four you can hear. Anxiety lives in the scary future; your senses drag you back to the actual now.

content.prompt: Put the cool-down in the order that works.
steps: Notice the early signs — heat, tight chest, racing thoughts
Slow your breathing with a long exhale
Step back from the moment instead of reacting
Reappraise — is my story about this even accurate?
content.explanation: Notice first or you'll miss the window; breathe and step back to drain the flood; then your calmer brain can check the story.

content.prompt: Match each reset to what it calms.
Does this cool you down or fuel the fire?

items.text: Replaying the insult on a loop
Splashing cold water on your face
Reading your story as the only truth

content.prompt: What's your earliest body sign that you're heating up — before you even feel 'angry'?
content.context: Spotting it early is the whole game. The sooner you catch the flood, the easier it is to step out of it.
content.explanation: Your body sounds the alarm before your brain labels it. Learn your tell, and you get a head start on cooling down.

content.prompt: Best way to settle a spike of anxiety in the moment?
content.context: Your heart is racing five minutes before you have to present.
options: Take fast, shallow breaths to match how you feel
Slow, long exhales and notice three things in the room
Replay every possible way it could go wrong
Chug a big energy drink to power through it
content.explanation: Long exhales tell your nervous system you're safe, and grounding in your senses pulls you out of the scary what-ifs.

content.title: In the heat
content.intro: A teammate posts in the group chat blaming you for the missed deadline — and it wasn't even your part. You're furious and panicking. Keep your cool head above 50.

steps.situation: The message lands. Your face is hot and your thumbs are already moving.

options.text: Put the phone down and take three slow breaths
options.feedback: You bought yourself time before the flood could pick the words.

options.text: Fire back 'are you serious right now??' instantly
options.feedback: Hot reply sent. Now it's a fight, and you started it on adrenaline.

options.text: Type a long paragraph and reread it ten times
options.feedback: Stewing in it keeps the flood topped up instead of draining.

steps.situation: You're calmer. The story in your head is 'they're trying to make me look bad.'

options.text: Decide they definitely did it on purpose to wreck you
options.feedback: The inflated story keeps the anger burning on assumptions.

options.text: Check the story — maybe they're stressed and got the facts wrong
options.feedback: Reappraising deflates the worst-case story before you act on it.

options.text: Spiral into 'everyone's going to think it was me'
options.feedback: Anxiety inflates the threat — now you're fighting a disaster that hasn't happened.

steps.situation: Time to respond. What goes out?

options.text: A sarcastic 'wow, nice of you to blame me' to the whole chat
options.feedback: Sarcasm reads as an attack and drags everyone into the fire.

options.text: Nothing — go silent and let it eat at you for days
options.feedback: Burying it doesn't cool it; it just moves the heat inward.

options.text: A calm message: the timeline shows your part was done — let's sort it
options.feedback: Cool, clear, fixable. You named the facts without an attack.

content.outro: Same unfair moment, two completely different endings. The cool head wasn't born calm — it breathed, checked the story, then spoke.
